name: Login
description: Standalone /login route, exercised UNauthenticated via the Spec CI unauth-context lane (metadata.requiresUnauthenticated). Structural state asserts the sign-in form: the email/username input (#login-username), the password input (#login-password), and the 'Sign In' submit button. The behavioral transition types ci-bot's email + the {{SPEC_CI_AUTH_PASSWORD}} secret placeholder (substituted at runtime from the GHA secret — never committed) and clicks Sign In, then waits for the login form to vanish (the credentials were accepted and /login redirected to the mode-aware authenticated home). Login is non-destructive: it only establishes a session in the throwaway unauth context, which is torn down after the spec.

states.name: Sign-in form
states.description: The /login sign-in form: email-or-username input, password input, and the Sign In submit button. Matched by the DOM id attribute (stable, author-set) rather than the bridge-assigned element id.

assertions.description: Sign In submit button is present. Disambiguated from the marketing-header 'Sign In' button (which opens the auth dialog, type=null) by attributes.type=submit — the form's <button type=submit>.

transitions.name: Sign in with valid credentials
transitions.description: Behavioral: type ci-bot's email into #login-username, type the {{SPEC_CI_AUTH_PASSWORD}} secret (runtime-substituted from the GHA secret, never committed) into #login-password, then click the form's Sign In submit button (type=submit, disambiguated from the marketing-header Sign In). The waitAfter IS the behavioral assertion: the login input (#login-username) VANISHES — i.e. the credentials were accepted and /login client-side-redirected to the mode-aware authenticated home (Spec CI forces Visual mode -> /tools/visual-automation), so the login form unmounts and deregisters. Vanish is used (not 'authenticated nav appears') because the post-login destination differs by product mode, but the login form unmounting is a stable cross-mode success signal that survives the client-side (context-preserving) navigation. A failed login keeps the form mounted (it surfaces a toast, not a redirect), so vanish is a true success discriminator. activateStates is empty because the post-login surface is a different route than the structural login-form state; encoding it as a second always-required state would force a structural partial_match (it can't exist on the /login page the matcher first snapshots). effect:read because it only establishes a session in the throwaway unauth context (torn down after the spec); it mutates no server state.
